summary: I was born in India in 1998, even though my parents had moved to California earlier. I moved back and forth between India and the US several times during my childhood, which made me question my identity. By the time I started high school in India, I felt settled and had embraced being an Indian with an American passport. India has always been a big part of my life, from the languages I speak to the traditions my family follows.

My parents came to California when my dad joined Microsoft in 1994, and I spent my elementary school years in Fremont, California. Growing up there, I balanced my American school life with Indian cultural practices at home. Watching shows like Arthur and Sesame Street on PBS Kids was part of my daily routine. California was where my American experience began, even though I was born in India.

My dad joined Microsoft in 1994, which was the reason my family initially moved to California. His job there shaped much of our early life in the US. Microsoft was indirectly responsible for my upbringing in California and the multicultural environment I grew up in. It was part of the backdrop to my family's American dream.

I recited ancient Sanskrit shlokas in the evenings while attending elementary school in California. Sanskrit was part of my cultural education at home, connecting me to my Indian heritage. It was one of the three languages I grew up speaking and learning, which helped shape my identity. Sanskrit gave me a link to the traditions and history of India even while living in the US.

After school on weekdays, I used to watch Arthur on PBS Kids, which was part of my American childhood experience. It was one of the shows that helped me connect with American culture while balancing my Indian roots. Watching Arthur was a daily routine that made my school days feel familiar and fun. It was a small but meaningful part of growing up in California.

Sesame Street was another show I watched regularly after school on PBS Kids during my elementary years in California. It helped me learn about American culture and language in a fun way. Alongside Arthur, Sesame Street was part of my daily routine and contributed to my bilingual and bicultural upbringing. It balanced well with the Indian traditions I practiced at home.

PBS Kids was the channel where I watched shows like Arthur and Sesame Street after school during my childhood in California. It was a key part of my American cultural exposure and education. Through PBS Kids, I connected with American childhood experiences while still maintaining my Indian identity at home. It was a comforting and familiar part of my daily routine.

Our house was always filled with old Bollywood and Indian classical music, which kept me connected to Indian culture even while living in the US. Bollywood was a big part of my family's cultural background and influenced the atmosphere at home. It was one of the ways my parents maintained our Indian roots despite being in America. Bollywood music and films were a comforting reminder of home.

Diwali was our biggest holiday growing up, celebrated with much enthusiasm in our family. It was more important to us than Christmas, although my mom still brought home a little tinsel tree for the holidays. Diwali celebrations helped me feel connected to my Indian heritage and community, even while living in California. It was a vibrant reminder of where I came from.

I knew the Indian national anthem from a young age, which was part of my upbringing in a bicultural household. Alongside this, I also learned the American Pledge of Allegiance, reflecting my dual identity. The anthem connected me to India and its culture, even while living in the US. It was a symbol of my roots and heritage.

I learned the American Pledge of Allegiance in two languages, which was part of my experience growing up between India and the US. Reciting it alongside the Indian national anthem highlighted my unique cultural blend. The Pledge was a daily reminder of my American identity while balancing my Indian roots. It symbolized the duality of my upbringing.

I started to really question my identity when I set foot on the University of Michigan campus. It was there that I began asking my new friends about American culture and history, trying to understand what it means to be Indian American. The University of Michigan marked a new chapter where I explored my bicultural identity more deeply. It was a place of self-discovery and growth.

I often wonder what it truly means to be Indian American, especially after moving between India and the US multiple times. My experiences have shaped my understanding of this identity, but I question if it can be described by a single label. Indian Americans like me navigate a blend of cultures, traditions, and expectations from both countries. It's a complex but beautiful part of who I am.
